# Diffusion Policy {.section-divider}

## What Diffusion Policy Generates

Diffusion Policy generates short-horizon action trajectories:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\mathbf{a}_{t:t+H}
\sim
p_\theta(\mathbf{a}_{t:t+H} \mid \mathbf{o}_{t-T_o:t})
$$
:::

This gives the policy temporal structure beyond a single next action.

::: {.notes}
Main point: Diffusion Policy models a distribution over action chunks conditioned on recent observations.

Intuition: the chunk captures how actions should unfold over a short horizon, not just the next motor command.

Warning: do not confuse the action horizon $H$ with the diffusion denoising horizon; they are different loops.
:::

---

## Action Trajectories Instead of Single Actions

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- A trajectory can encode approach, contact, and recovery phases.
- Temporal action chunks reduce myopic behavior.
- Multimodal sequence generation can represent several plausible strategies.
:::

::: {.figure-placeholder}
<div class="placeholder-title">Action trajectory chunk</div>
<div class="placeholder-path">figures/action_trajectory_chunk.png</div>
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: action trajectories let the policy express short-term temporal intent.

Intuition: opening a drawer is not a sequence of independent actions; approach, contact, pull, and recovery are coupled.

Warning: a longer chunk is not always better because stale plans may ignore new contact information.
:::

---

## Observation Conditioning

Possible conditioning inputs:

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- RGB or RGB-D images
- proprioception
- force/torque measurements
- task labels or language instructions
:::

::: {.figure-placeholder}
<div class="placeholder-title">Observation encoder diagram</div>
<div class="placeholder-path">figures/observation_conditioning.png</div>
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: conditioning determines what information the denoiser can use to choose a plausible action trajectory.

Intuition: vision may tell the object pose, proprioception tells the robot configuration, and force tells whether contact has occurred.

Warning: adding sensors does not automatically help; timing, calibration, and representation quality matter.
:::

---

## Action Chunking

- Predict $H$ future actions.
- Execute only the first $h \le H$ actions.
- Replan with fresh observations.

This connects diffusion sampling with receding horizon control.

::: {.notes}
Main point: action chunking is the bridge between slow generative sampling and continuous robot execution.

Intuition: sample a short plan, execute only the beginning, then update with fresh observations.

Warning: students may assume the whole sampled chunk is executed; in receding horizon execution, much of it can be discarded.
:::

---

## Receding Horizon Execution

At robot time $t$:

::: {.process-list}
1. observe the scene
2. sample an action trajectory
3. execute the first actions
4. discard the remainder
5. replan
:::

::: {.figure-placeholder}
<div class="placeholder-title">Receding horizon timeline</div>
<div class="placeholder-path">figures/receding_horizon_execution.png</div>
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: emphasize the closed-loop nature of deployment.

Intuition: this is similar to model predictive control: plan ahead, execute a prefix, replan.

Warning: the policy is not purely open-loop just because it outputs a sequence; replanning frequency is essential.
:::

---

## Training Pipeline

- Collect demonstrations.
- Encode observations.
- Add noise to expert action chunks.
- Train the denoising network.

::: {.figure-placeholder}
<div class="placeholder-title">Diffusion Policy training pipeline</div>
<div class="placeholder-path">figures/diffusion_policy_training.png</div>
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: training denoises expert action chunks conditioned on observations.

Intuition: the model sees a corrupted expert trajectory and learns how to recover the original expert-like chunk.

Warning: training quality depends heavily on demonstration diversity and alignment between observations and actions.
:::

---

## Inference Pipeline

- Encode current observations.
- Initialize noisy action trajectory.
- Iteratively denoise.
- Send selected actions to the controller.

::: {.figure-placeholder}
<div class="placeholder-title">Diffusion Policy inference pipeline</div>
<div class="placeholder-path">figures/diffusion_policy_inference.png</div>
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: inference starts from noise and repeatedly denoises toward an executable action chunk.

Intuition: the policy constructs an action sequence through iterative refinement rather than a single forward prediction.

Warning: this iterative process is exactly why latency becomes a design constraint for robot control.
:::

---

## Demonstration Video Placeholder

::: {.figure-placeholder}
<div class="placeholder-title">Diffusion Policy robot demo video</div>
<div class="placeholder-path">assets/videos/diffusion_policy_demo.mp4</div>
:::

```{=html}
<!-- Example future syntax:
<video controls src="assets/videos/diffusion_policy_demo.mp4"></video>
-->
```

::: {.notes}
Main point: use the demo to connect the abstract pipeline to visible robot behavior.

Intuition: ask students to watch for temporal coherence, recovery after small disturbances, and contact transitions.

Warning: videos can hide failures and reset costs; do not treat a successful clip as complete evidence of robustness.
:::
